title: Polo the Penguin and the Test

content.problemStatement:
Read problems statements in Russian herePolo, the Penguin, has a lot of tests tomorrow at the university.

He knows that there are **N** different questions that will be on the tests. For each question **i** (**i = 1..N**), he knows **C[i]** - the number of tests that will contain this question, **P[i]** - the number of points that he will get for correctly answering this question on each of tests and **T[i]** - the amount of time (in minutes) that he needs to spend to learn this question.


Unfortunately, the amount of free time that Polo has is limited to **W** minutes. Help him to find the maximal possible total number of points he can get for all tests if he studies for no more than **W** minutes.
content.inputFormat: First line contains T, the number of test cases. Each test case starts with a line containing two integers N and W, where N is the number of questions and W is the total time available. The next N lines contain three integers each: C[i], P[i], and T[i], representing the number of tests containing question i, the points for correctly answering question i, and the time needed to learn question i, respectively.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output the maximum possible total points Polo can get for all tests if he studies for no more than W minutes.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The best choice is to learn the first and the third questions and get 1*2 + 3*3 = 11 points.

The best choice is to learn the first question and get 1*3 = 3 points, and then learn the second question and get 2*4 = 8 points, but since we have only 5 minutes, we can only learn the second question and get 2*4 = 8 points, however, the time required to learn both questions exceeds the available time, so we choose the second question only.
Since there is only one question and we have enough time to learn it, we learn it and get 5*10 = 50 points.

solution.approach: This problem can be solved using the 0/1 Knapsack algorithm, a classic problem in Dynamic Programming. The idea is to create a 2D table where each cell [i][w] represents the maximum value that can be obtained with i items and a capacity of w. We fill this table in a bottom-up manner, considering whether to include or exclude the current item at each step.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N * W) per test case, O(T * N * W) total. The precomputation is done once for each test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N * W) — the dominant structure is the 2D table used for Dynamic Programming.
